content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 958 I 4-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David Bauer 
2150 River Plaza Dr., #150 
Sacramento, CA 95833 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-121 
Dear Mr. Bauer: 
August 18, 2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, the Commission is charged 
with interpreting and enforcing the provisions of the Act, and may provide advice only regarding 
those provisions. (Section 83114.) 
QUESTION 
Does the Act permit a general purpose committee to invest a portion of its savings by 
buying an annuity contract through a life insurance company? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. There is no proscription in the Act preventing a general purpose committee from 
investing a portion of its holdings. 
FACTS 
You are a treasurer for Sportsman's Political Victory Fund, a general purpose recipient 
committee. The board for this committee wishes to maximize the return on its cash reserves. To 
receive a greater return than would be possible with a bank vehicle, such as a certificate of 
deposit or money market account, the board would like to purchase an annuity contract from a 
life insurance company. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-11-121 
Page No. 2 
A "general purpose" committee, as defined in Section 82027 .5 means any committee that 
receives $1,000 in contributions in a calendar year that is formed or exists primarily to support or 
oppose more than one candidate or ballot measure. A general purpose committee also means a 
committee that makes independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more per calendar year, or 
makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more per calendar year to or at the behest of candidates 
or committees. 
The Act provides guidance regarding how committee funds must be used in Sections 
89510-89519. Specifically, Section 89512.5 states that expenditures by a committee must be 
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. Additionally, an expenditure that 
confers a substantial personal benefit on an individual or individuals with authority to approve 
the expenditure of campaign funds must be directly related to a political, legislative, or 
governmental purpose. The Act also states that committee funds may not be commingled with 
any individual's personal funds. (Section 84307.) 
While the Act provides several restrictions on the ways in which a candidate controlled or 
candidate committee can establish its bank account and invest campaign funds (Section 85201; 
Regulation 18524), there is nothing in the Act that establishes requirements for a general purpose 
committee's bank account. For record-keeping purposes, however, before making any 
expenditure, the committee should return the funds to the committee bank account so that all 
expenditures are properly recorded. (Section 84104; Regulation 18401.) 
The Act does not prohibit the Sportsman's Political Victory Fund from investing its 
holdings by purchasing an annuity contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
dlPi 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Counsel, Legal Division

buying an annuity contract through a life insurance company?

CONCLUSION: Yes. There is no proscription in the Act preventing a general purpose committee from
